key_insight: Under a nanoscratch contact in 316H stainless steel, the martensitic transformation pathway depends on local stress state: hydrostatic compression favors γ→ε, while shear relief enables ε→α′, producing a sequential γ→ε→α′ tribolayer.
engineering_relevance: These spatially resolved insights explain how frictional loading alters near-surface microstructure and can help guide alloy design for improved wear and galling resistance.
method: In-situ synchrotron X-ray nanodiffractometry during nanoscratch testing
Spatial mapping of strain and phase evolution beneath a single sliding asperity
Finite element modeling to interpret stress-driven phase selection
evidence_signal: The abstract reports spatial maps of evolving strain and phase distributions that reveal distinct martensitic pathways and correlate them with local constraint conditions.
watch_item: The results come from a single-asperity nanoscratch setup, so direct transfer to multi-asperity, real-service tribological contacts should be done cautiously.
paper_title: Spatially resolved in-situ characterisation of competing martensitic transformation pathways during nanoscratch in 316H Stainless Steel
